entries.title: Functional-only learning app provides no retention hook
entries.description: A learning app that correctly displays questions and answers but lacks emotional engagement, progress visibility, and motivational feedback will not retain users. Functional correctness is necessary but insufficient for a product users return to voluntarily.
entries.context: AskFin prototype provided accurate question analysis and learning modes but felt like a tool, not a coach. Users had no reason to return daily without external motivation.
entries.lesson: Define the emotional core of the product before building features. Ask: why would a user open this tomorrow without being told to?

entries.title: Emotional micro-copy outperforms data-only feedback
entries.description: Contextual, emotionally aware feedback text drives stronger user engagement than raw data display. 'Stark! 8 von 10 richtig' outperforms 'Score: 80%'. Feedback after correct answers should differ from feedback after incorrect answers.
entries.context: Identified during AskFin premium reframing. Generic score displays were emotionally flat. Reframing to contextual micro-copy with personality made the same data feel like coaching.

entries.title: Domain-specific progress tracking beats generic gamification
entries.description: A progress metric tied to the user's real goal (e.g. exam readiness score 0-100%) is more motivating than generic points, badges, or XP systems. Users care about 'Am I ready for my exam?' not 'How many points do I have?'.
entries.context: AskFin reframing replaced abstract score tracking with a Pruefungsbereitschafts-Score (exam readiness percentage) and a Skill Map showing topic-level competence. This reframing changed the product from a question tool to a coaching system.
entries.effect: Transforms utility tool into goal-oriented coaching experience. User sees meaningful progress toward their actual objective.

entries.title: Reset SelectorGroupChat between pipeline passes to prevent context explosion
entries.description: AutoGen SelectorGroupChat accumulates all messages across team.run() calls in _message_thread, _message_buffer, and agent _model_context. After a heavy pass (25-40 generated files), accumulated context exceeds Haiku's 50k input token/min rate limit. Calling team.reset() between passes clears all accumulated state.
entries.context: DriveAI pipeline crashed consistently at Bug Hunter Pass (first pass after Implementation) with anthropic.RateLimitError 429. Root cause: 60-80k tokens accumulated from Implementation Pass sent to Haiku in a single request.

entries.title: Compact implementation summary restores review quality after context reset
entries.description: After team.reset() clears context between passes, review agents lose all implementation detail. A deterministic summary (~300-2000 chars) built from extraction metadata (file names, type keywords, architectural patterns) restores enough context for file-specific reviews without reintroducing token pressure.
entries.context: After implementing FK-004 (context reset), Bug Hunter and Refactor passes gave generic feedback disconnected from generated code. Adding a compact summary prepended to review task prompts restored file-specific bug detection and refactoring suggestions.

entries.title: New review agents should start as advisory-only passes
entries.description: When introducing a new review agent (e.g. Creative Director), start it as advisory-only with strict message limits (MaxMessageTermination=2) and no veto power. This validates the agent's value without risking pipeline stability. Promote to blocking gate only after the agent consistently produces actionable feedback.
entries.context: Creative Director was introduced as a separate review pass after Bug Hunter, advisory only, with MaxMessageTermination(2). This prevented runaway conversations and allowed safe validation. The CD produced useful product-level feedback without destabilizing the pipeline.

entries.title: Answer feedback must explain WHY, not just show correct/incorrect
entries.description: Showing only correct/incorrect after a user answers a question produces shallow learning. Adding a 1-2 sentence explanation of WHY the answer is correct activates elaborative processing and significantly improves long-term retention. The explanation should reference the underlying rule or principle, not just restate the correct option.
entries.context: UX Psychology review of ExamSimulation screen found that ResultScreenView showed binary correct/incorrect feedback without reasoning. Research on the testing effect (Roediger & Butler, 2011) shows retrieval + elaboration doubles retention compared to retrieval alone.
entries.lesson: Every answer reveal should include a brief explanation connecting the correct answer to the underlying principle. Format: 'Richtig, weil [Regel/Prinzip]' or 'Die richtige Antwort ist X, weil [Grund]'.

entries.title: Show competence progress between tasks, not only at the end
entries.description: Users who receive no competence signal between individual tasks (questions, exercises, challenges) lose intrinsic motivation and feel uncertain about their readiness. A brief mid-session checkpoint showing accuracy, strengths, and gaps maintains agency and supports self-regulation. This is grounded in Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan): competence is a core psychological need.
entries.context: UX Psychology review found ExamSimulation transitioning between questions without any competence signal. Users could not gauge readiness during a session, only after completion. SDT research shows real-time competence feedback sustains engagement better than delayed summary feedback.
entries.lesson: After each task/question, show a brief competence signal: current accuracy, topic being tested, whether it is a strength or gap. Keep it lightweight (one line, not a full dashboard).

entries.title: Visually differentiate task types to reduce cognitive load
entries.description: When all tasks (questions, challenges, exercises) look identical, users cannot activate appropriate mental schemas before engaging. This increases extraneous cognitive load and error rates. Subtle visual or textual differentiation (e.g. micro-labels like 'Recall: Traffic Signs' vs. 'Application: Hazard Scenario') helps users deploy the right reasoning strategy. Based on Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller, 1988).
entries.context: UX Psychology review found ExamSimulation presenting all question types in identical format. Users had no cue whether they faced a recall question, application question, or hazard perception task. Cognitive Load Theory shows that reducing extraneous load frees working memory for germane processing.
entries.lesson: Add a subtle type indicator (micro-label or icon) to each task so users know what kind of thinking is required before they engage. This is not visual decoration — it is a cognitive scaffolding mechanism.

entries.title: Interleave weak topics within sessions using spacing principles
entries.description: Massed practice (repeating the same topic consecutively) creates illusions of competence that fade within 24 hours. Interleaving weak topics with mastered ones within a single session — and spacing re-exposure across sessions at expanding intervals (1-3-7 days) — produces durable learning. Based on the spacing effect (Ebbinghaus, 1885; Cepeda et al., 2006).
entries.context: UX Psychology review found ExamSimulation generating questions sequentially without spacing. If a user got a traffic sign question wrong in minute 5, they would not see another traffic sign question until the next session, missing the critical re-exposure window.
entries.lesson: When a user gets a topic wrong, re-expose them to a different question from the same topic within the same session (3-8 questions later). Across sessions, use expanding intervals (1 day, 3 days, 7 days) for weak topics.

entries.title: AI review text embedded inside generated source files
entries.pattern: AI agents writing code reach a token limit or context boundary and emit review/analysis text (Markdown headings, bullet points, commentary like 'I'm stopping here intentionally...') directly into the source file instead of terminating the code block cleanly.
entries.failure_mode: Swift compiler encounters Markdown syntax (#, ---, **) or English prose inside .swift files. Results in dozens of cascading compile errors. Entire file may be unusable.
entries.prevention_rule: After code extraction, validate every .swift file: (1) contains no Markdown markers (lines starting with #, ---, **) outside of string literals, (2) has balanced braces {}, (3) first non-comment line is import/struct/class/enum/protocol/extension/func. Reject files that fail.
entries.evidence_summary: 5 files affected in askfin_v1-1: TrainingSessionManager.swift (BUG-CR-001), QuestionCard.swift (FIX-BUILD-001), TrainingModeViewModel.swift (FIX-BUILD-002), LearningStreak+Display.swift (FIX-BUILD-003), TrainingModeScreen.swift (FIX-BUILD-004). All had valid Swift code followed by AI review prose.
entries.when_to_apply: Post-generation validation of every source file before writing to disk
entries.when_not_to_apply: Files that are intentionally Markdown (.md) or documentation

entries.title: Duplicate type definitions across multiple generated files
entries.pattern: Multiple agents or generation passes independently create the same type (struct, enum, class) in different files. Common variant: an aggregate file (e.g. TrainingModels.swift) defines types that also exist as standalone files (SpacingItem.swift, TrainingSession.swift). Another variant: a type is defined in both Models/ and ViewModels/ or Services/.
entries.failure_mode: Swift compiler error: 'invalid redeclaration of [TypeName]'. Affects every file that imports the type. One redeclaration can cascade into 10+ errors.
entries.prevention_rule: Maintain a type registry during generation. Before creating a new type definition, check if the type name already exists in any generated file. Rule: one type = one file = one canonical path. Never define types inside aggregate 'kitchen sink' files when standalone files exist.
entries.evidence_summary: 8+ redeclarations in askfin_v1-1: SpacingItem (BUG-CR-002), TrainingSession + SessionType (BUG-CR-005), TrainingSessionManager at two paths (BUG-CR-008), SessionType in ViewModel (FIX-BUILD-005), Question/Category/SessionResult cross-layer (FIX-BUILD-006), StatCard (FIX-BUILD-013), SessionPhase Equatable (BUG-CR-004).
entries.when_to_apply: Any multi-file generation run, especially when multiple agents create files independently
entries.when_not_to_apply: Intentional protocol default implementations or conditional compilation blocks

entries.title: Generated call sites use wrong parameter names or missing parameters
entries.pattern: Agent A generates a View or Service with specific init/function parameter names. Agent B (or a later pass) generates code that calls that View/Service but uses different parameter names, adds parameters that don't exist, or omits required parameters. Root cause: agents generate producer and consumer code without cross-referencing actual signatures.
entries.failure_mode: Swift compiler errors: 'incorrect argument label', 'missing argument for parameter', 'extra argument in call'. Each mismatch is a separate compile error.
entries.prevention_rule: When generating a call site for a type defined in another file, the generator must reference the actual init/function signature from the target file. Never guess parameter names from context. If the target file doesn't exist yet, generate both producer and consumer in the same pass.
entries.detection_hint: Build the project — parameter mismatches produce highly specific compiler errors with exact expected vs actual labels.
entries.evidence_summary: 4 View call-site mismatches in askfin_v1-1 (FIX-BUILD-014): SessionBriefView onStart→onDismiss, QuestionCardView onRevealOptions→onRevealTap, SessionSummaryView missing preSessionLevels, AnswerRevealView missing 4 parameters. Also FIX-BUILD-015: SessionSummaryView used non-existent properties (totalCount, correctCount, results).
entries.when_to_apply: Whenever generated code references types or functions defined in other generated files
entries.when_not_to_apply: Self-contained single-file generation where all types are local

entries.title: Referenced types, protocols, or service methods not generated
entries.pattern: Generated code references types (structs, enums, protocols) or service methods that were never created. Happens when agent designs an interface in one file but the implementation or supporting types were planned for a later pass that never ran, timed out, or hit MAX_FILES_PER_RUN.
entries.failure_mode: Swift compiler: 'cannot find type [X] in scope', 'value of type [Y] has no member [Z]'. Can cascade: one missing type breaks every file that imports it.
entries.prevention_rule: After generation completes, build a dependency graph: for every type referenced, verify it exists as a definition in some generated file. Flag any unresolved references before writing output. Critical missing types must be stubbed at minimum.
entries.detection_hint: xcodebuild 2>&1 | grep 'cannot find type\|has no member' | sort -u
entries.evidence_summary: Multiple instances in askfin_v1-1: TrainingResult + CategoryStats never generated (BUG-CR-003), ExamCountdown + ProgressSummary + StreakData + QuizSession missing (FIX-BUILD-007), InMemoryPersistenceStore + MockQuestionBank + DashboardDataService missing (FIX-BUILD-010), 6 Dashboard sub-views referenced but never created (FIX-BUILD-016), TopicCompetenceService missing 5+ methods (FIX-BUILD-009).
entries.when_to_apply: Every generation run, especially when MAX_FILES_PER_RUN may truncate output
entries.when_not_to_apply: When referencing Apple SDK types or third-party frameworks

entries.title: Bundle.module used in regular Xcode app targets
entries.pattern: Generated SwiftUI code uses NSLocalizedString with Bundle.module or Text("key", bundle: .module) for localization. Bundle.module is only available in Swift Package Manager targets, not in regular Xcode app targets.
entries.failure_mode: Swift compiler: 'type Bundle has no member module'. Affects every file using localized strings this way.
entries.prevention_rule: For regular Xcode app targets: use inline strings directly (e.g. Text("Dashboard")) or String(localized:) with Bundle.main. Only use Bundle.module when explicitly generating a Swift Package. The project type must be known before generation starts.
entries.evidence_summary: 5 files affected in askfin_v1-1 (FIX-BUILD-012): PremiumDashboardViewModel, ProgressGridCard, StreakIndicator, PremiumDashboardView, ExamCountdownCard. All used Bundle.module in a regular Xcode app target.
entries.when_to_apply: Any iOS/macOS app generation that is not a Swift Package
entries.when_not_to_apply: Swift Package Manager library targets where Bundle.module is correct

entries.title: Custom init in struct body suppresses Swift memberwise initializer
entries.pattern: A generated struct defines a custom convenience init() inside the struct body. Swift's rule: any explicit init in the struct body suppresses the automatic memberwise init. Other generated code that relies on the memberwise init then fails to compile.
entries.failure_mode: Swift compiler: 'extra argument [X] in call' or 'missing argument for parameter [Y]' at every call site using the memberwise init pattern.
entries.prevention_rule: When generating a struct that needs both a custom init and the default memberwise init, always place the custom init in an extension. Rule: `extension MyStruct { init(convenience params...) { } }`. This preserves the auto-generated memberwise init.
entries.detection_hint: Look for structs with stored properties AND an init() in the same body — then check if any call site uses the memberwise pattern.
entries.evidence_summary: TrainingSession in askfin_v1-1 (FIX-BUILD-017): convenience init defined in struct body suppressed memberwise init. TrainingModeViewModel could not call TrainingSession(id:categoryId:categoryName:startedAt:). Fix: moved convenience init to extension.
entries.when_to_apply: Any Swift struct generation where both custom and memberwise init are needed
entries.when_not_to_apply: Classes (which don't have automatic memberwise inits) or structs that intentionally replace the memberwise init

entries.title: Feature-layer type name collisions between app versions or modules
entries.pattern: When generating a new feature layer (e.g. Premium) alongside an existing layer (e.g. MVP), both layers independently define common type names like Question, Category, SessionResult. Without module boundaries or namespacing, these collide in Swift's flat namespace within a single target.
entries.failure_mode: Swift compiler: 'ambiguous use of [TypeName]' or 'invalid redeclaration'. Affects every file that references the ambiguous type. Requires renaming across 10-20+ files.
entries.prevention_rule: When generating code for a new feature layer that coexists with existing code in the same target, prefix domain-specific types with the layer name (e.g. PremiumQuestion, PremiumCategory). Alternatively, use Swift module boundaries. Never reuse generic type names (Question, Result, Category, Item, Session) across layers without namespacing.
entries.detection_hint: grep -rn '^struct Question\b\|^struct Category\b\|^struct Session\b' across all source directories
entries.evidence_summary: 3 type collisions in askfin_v1-1 (FIX-BUILD-006): Question→PremiumQuestion, Category→PremiumCategory, SessionResult→PracticeSessionResult. Required updates in ~15 files across Services, ViewModels, and Models.
entries.when_to_apply: Any project with multiple feature layers, app versions, or modules sharing one Xcode target
entries.when_not_to_apply: Single-layer apps or projects using proper Swift module separation

entries.title: File duplication in code generation output
entries.description: Code generation produced duplicate files: DashboardView.swift (4 copies). Extraction pipeline needs deduplication.
entries.lesson: Code generation produced duplicate files: DashboardView.swift (4 copies). Extraction pipeline needs deduplication.

entries.title: SwiftUI lifecycle pattern: memory leak
entries.description: Generated SwiftUI code missing lifecycle management: memory leak
entries.context: Auto-promoted: observed in 2 proposals across runs 20260313_092851, 20260314_182358.
entries.lesson: Generated SwiftUI code missing lifecycle management: memory leak

entries.content: Haiku model generates architecture discussion instead of extractable code for Domain/Service layer tasks. Use Sonnet (standard profile) for Domain layer generation.

Layered build order successfully prevents FK-014 cascade: Foundation types exist before Domain references them. 5-layer sequence (Foundation->Domain->Application->Presentation->Polish) validated for Android/Kotlin.
Kotlin ProjectIntegrator needs content-aware routing: @Composable files must route to Views/, not Models/. File extension alone is insufficient for Kotlin/TypeScript — read file content for routing.

GeneratedHelpers files are junk containers across all languages (Swift, Kotlin, TypeScript). The CodeExtractor creates them as fallback when it cannot determine a proper filename. They should be auto-quarantined in the Assembly phase.

RepairEngine reduces TypeScript errors in 3 waves: (1) MissingImportFixer for cross-file refs (25%), (2) TypeAnnotationFixer for implicit-any + MissingTypeFixer for stubs (50%), (3) ModulePathFixer for @/ aliases. All three waves together fix 96% of typical Haiku-generated TypeScript errors (99 -> 4).

entries.title: Product quality gap: Generic output detected
entries.description: Creative Director rated output as 'fail'. Key issue: Generic output detected
entries.context: Auto-promoted: observed in 2 proposals across runs 20260319_163314, 20260320_161115.
entries.lesson: Creative Director rated output as 'fail'. Key issue: Generic output detected
